input.incident: Forgot my coworker Sarah's birthday; need a thoughtful repair message and gift idea.

giftIdeas.title: Same-day flowers or local florist bouquet
giftIdeas.whyItFits: Neutral and widely appropriate; search locally rather than guessing inventory.

giftIdeas.title: Premium chocolate or snack gift box
giftIdeas.whyItFits: Coworker-safe when interests are unknown; easy to verify via search.

giftIdeas.title: Small desk plant or succulent
giftIdeas.whyItFits: Low-risk tone; avoids strong personal taste assumptions.

plan: Acknowledge what went wrong without deflecting.
Offer one specific repair action (time, place, or gesture).
Ask what they need to feel respected.
Follow up once after a pause—no pressure.
risks: They may need time before responding.
Long or repeated messages can feel like pressure.

writing.subject: Re: forgot birthday
writing.apologyMessage:
Hi,

I'm truly sorry—I messed up, and I'm sorry for the hurt it caused. I'd like to make this right; tell me what would help, or I can suggest a concrete next step on my end.
writing.followUpMessage:
Hi,

I'll give you space. When you're ready, I'm here—no pressure to reply quickly.

email.subject: Re: forgot birthday
email.body:
Hi,

I'm truly sorry—I messed up, and I'm sorry for the hurt it caused. I'd like to make this right; tell me what would help, or I can suggest a concrete next step on my end.

—

Hi,

I'll give you space. When you're ready, I'm here—no pressure to reply quickly.

followup.title: Follow up: forgot birthday
followup.notes:
• No excuses; center impact on them.
• Keep it short.
• Include one concrete repair action.
• Do not invent facts or detailed alibis.

Plan: Acknowledge what went wrong without deflecting. Offer one specific repair action (time, place, or gesture). Ask what they need to feel respected. Follow up once after a pause—no pressure.
